reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Closely follows the example page's structure: noun-form H2s, same relative ordering of shared sections, same frontmatter keys, and matching MDX component palette (Tip, Warning, CodeBlock, ReferenceableParams, ApiReference).
Opening paragraph and pricing line match the example's shape — self-contained, 40-50 words, definition-to-reason-to-care structure with a standalone pricing context sentence.
Standard vs FIFO comparison table is well-structured and covers all material differences (ordering, delivery, subscriber types, throughput, use cases).
Lambda subscription section mirrors the example's 'Triggering a Lambda function' section in depth: prose explaining key trigger properties, a working config example, and a realistic handler snippet.

Connecting to other resources includes a table of injected environment variables named correctly for the example resource (STP_NOTIFICATIONS_ARN, STP_NOTIFICATIONS_NAME), consistent with the connectTo JSDoc in __helpers.d.ts.

Sections that genuinely don't apply to SNS topics (Alarms, EventBridge integration, queue policies) are correctly omitted — SNS is absent from StpAlarmEnabledResource and SnsTopicProps has no events or policyStatements property.
FAQ has exactly 10 Q&A pairs spanning all three required categories: Stacktape-specific, AWS use-case, and choosing/comparison questions.
ApiReference uses the correct definitionName='SnsTopicProps' and is placed immediately after the Referenceable parameters section, matching the example's placement.
problems: The Warning callout states 'Stacktape enforces this constraint — attempting to add an SNS trigger from a Lambda function to a FIFO topic will result in a deployment error.' This is a Stacktape-specific behavioral claim (compile-time or deploy-time enforcement) not backed by the provided source files. The source only documents the AWS SNS constraint. The Warning should attribute this to AWS SNS, not Stacktape.

The SMS display name subsection (### SMS display name) is nested under '## Standard vs FIFO topics' but SMS is a standard-topic-only feature unrelated to the FIFO comparison. The placement under FIFO creates a misleading hierarchy suggesting SMS is a FIFO variant, when the section's own content says 'This only applies to standard topics — FIFO topics do not support SMS delivery.'

FIFO throughput numbers (~300 publishes/s, 3,000 with batching) appear in both the table and FAQ but are not present in the provided source files (sns-topic.d.ts or ai-docs). These are well-known AWS facts and the example SQS page uses analogous numbers with the same sourcing gap, so this is a low-severity concern.
optionalImprovements: Soften the FIFO Warning from 'Stacktape enforces this constraint' to 'AWS SNS does not support delivering FIFO topic messages to Lambda, email, SMS, or HTTP subscribers — only FIFO SQS queues are valid FIFO topic targets.' This grounds the constraint in AWS behavior rather than implying a Stacktape-specific validation step.

Move '### SMS display name' out of '## Standard vs FIFO topics' — either elevate it to its own H2 section ('SMS delivery') or reframe it as a sub-section of standard topics specifically (e.g., '### Customizing SMS sender name'). The current nesting implies it is related to the FIFO comparison rather than being a standard-topic configuration option.

Add a brief note in the Subscribing a Lambda function section showing a minimal filterPolicy example (even a two-line snippet) — the section mentions it but the reader has no pattern to copy. The SQS page shows similar trigger configuration inline; a filter example would improve practical usefulness for the common 'subscribe only to certain event types' scenario.

The 'Subscribing a Lambda function' H2 uses 'Subscribing' (gerund) while the example page uses 'Triggering' (gerund). Both are noun-form, so this is not a style violation, but for tighter sibling-page consistency the label 'Triggering a Lambda function' or 'Lambda subscription' would align more closely with the example's verb choice.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Matches the example page's form precisely: noun-form H2s, same ordering, same frontmatter keys, ApiReference at the bottom after Referenceable parameters.
Standard vs FIFO comparison table is accurate, well-structured, and sourced directly from the type definitions including the FIFO subscriber-type restriction.
connectTo env-var table uses concrete resource-name-dependent names (STP_NOTIFICATIONS_ARN, STP_NOTIFICATIONS_NAME) exactly matching the source's SnsTopic JSDoc.
All Stacktape config examples correctly use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}/>; application code (handler, SDK usage) is in plain fenced blocks.
FAQ section covers all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS service, comparison/choosing) and contains exactly 10 questions.
Correctly omits alarms and EventBridge sections because the source (alarms.d.ts StpAlarmEnabledResource, SnsTopicProps) does not support them on SNS topics.
Pricing context line after the opening paragraph matches the example page pattern.
problems: The Warning block claims 'Stacktape enforces this constraint — attempting to add an SNS trigger from a Lambda function to a FIFO topic will result in a deployment error.' This is unsupported by the source. The source only says it's an AWS constraint (FIFO topics can only deliver to FIFO SQS queues). The source does not show any Stacktape-level validation that would produce this error message. This is a Stacktape-specific behavioral claim with no source grounding.

The fan-out to SQS pattern — listed as the primary 'When to use' scenario and the #1 stated use case — has no code example anywhere on the page. The page shows Lambda subscriptions and connectTo for publishing, but never demonstrates how to wire an SNS topic to multiple SQS queues. This is the most common real-world SNS pattern and a reader looking to implement it will find no concrete config guidance.

The filterPolicy description ('Uses SNS subscription filter policy syntax. For content-based filtering, use EventBridge instead.') names the feature and points to an alternative but gives no concrete example and no link to where the policy syntax is documented. Readers who need filtering will leave the page without enough to act.

The 'SMS display name' subsection is very thin — one sentence of prose and a trivial 1-property CodeBlock. It provides no decision support: when should you set this? What is the effect when left unset? What are the limitations beyond the 11-character cap? Most readers will not need this feature, so it should either be enriched or folded into the API reference callout.
mandatoryFixes: Remove the unsupported Stacktape-specific claim from the Warning block: 'Stacktape enforces this constraint — attempting to add an SNS trigger from a Lambda function to a FIFO topic will result in a deployment error.' Replace it with the source-grounded AWS constraint: 'FIFO topics can only deliver to FIFO SQS queues. Email, SMS, and HTTP subscribers are not supported on FIFO topics — this is an AWS limitation.'

Add a fan-out to SQS code example. The 'When to use' section and FAQ both cite SNS → multiple SQS queues as the canonical use case, but there is no config example demonstrating it. Add a subsection (e.g., under a new H2 'Fan-out pattern' or as a second example within 'Subscribing a Lambda function') that shows an SnsTopic with two SqsQueue subscribers, each configured with an SNS event trigger on a Lambda that reads from the queue.
optionalImprovements: Add a one-sentence link for filterPolicy: 'See the [AWS SNS subscription filter policy documentation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sns/latest/dg/sns-subscription-filter-policies.html) for supported operators.' or link to the SNS trigger page (/configuration/triggers/sns-events) for a working example.

Either enrich the 'SMS display name' subsection with a decision-support sentence ('Useful when subscribers need to identify the sender at a glance; leave unset when SMS is not a delivery channel') or collapse it to a one-liner callout and point to the API Reference for details.

Add a note in the 'Subscribing a Lambda function' section clarifying that Lambda invocations from SNS are synchronous from SNS's perspective — if the Lambda returns an error, SNS will retry with exponential backoff. This retry behavior directly affects how readers design their handlers.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Strong answer-first paragraph — self-contained, 40–70 words, names the AWS service and the 'why' clearly.
Standard vs FIFO comparison table with concrete delivery, ordering, throughput, and use-case rows is highly citable and scannable.
All three source-grounded properties (fifoEnabled, contentBasedDeduplication, smsDisplayName) are documented with code examples and surrounding prose.
10-question FAQ mixes Stacktape-specific, AWS-service, and choosing/comparison questions as required.
Pricing context line is present as the second paragraph, matching the example page pattern exactly.
connectTo env var table uses the concrete resource name from the example (STP_NOTIFICATIONS_ARN, STP_NOTIFICATIONS_NAME), matching the source JSDoc.
One correctly placed <ApiReference definitionName="SnsTopicProps" /> at the bottom, matching example page placement.
MDX component palette matches the example exactly (<Tip>, <Warning>, no <Info> or others).
H2 ordering and noun-form heading style match the example page precisely.
Frontmatter shape matches example (title, order, seoTitle, seoDescription — no extra or missing keys).
problems: Unsupported Stacktape-specific enforcement claim in the <Warning> block: 'Stacktape enforces this constraint — attempting to add an SNS trigger from a Lambda function to a FIFO topic will result in a deployment error.' The source confirms the AWS-level restriction (FIFO topics only deliver to FIFO SQS queues) but nothing in the source files confirms that Stacktape validates or rejects this at config time. This is a source-grounding issue.

Throughput figures in the FIFO comparison table (~300 publishes/s, 3,000 with batching) are not present in any of the provided source files. They are well-known AWS facts, but the style guide says to include concrete numbers only when they appear in the source. These numbers are borrowed from the SQS Queue example page, not from SNS source.

Unexplained tunable values in the Lambda subscriber code example: memory: 512 and timeout: 30 appear without any surrounding prose explaining what they control or what the common alternatives are. The style guide requires 1–2 sentences per non-obvious tunable value.

The smsDisplayName H3 is nested under the 'Standard vs FIFO topics' H2, but smsDisplayName is a top-level SnsTopicProps property, not a variant of a FIFO/standard comparison. The placement is slightly misleading — a reader scanning under that H2 expects FIFO-comparison content, not an unrelated SMS branding knob.

Decision support for smsDisplayName is thin — the section jumps from a one-sentence description to a code example with no guidance on when developers should set this vs leave it unset (e.g., only relevant when sending SMS; branding matters for regulated industries or consumer-facing apps).
optionalImprovements: Remove or narrow the Stacktape-enforcement claim in the <Warning>. Replace 'Stacktape enforces this constraint — attempting to add an SNS trigger from a Lambda function to a FIFO topic will result in a deployment error.' with something grounded in the AWS constraint: 'AWS enforces this at the service level — only FIFO SQS queues can subscribe to FIFO topics. Lambda, email, SMS, and HTTP subscribers are not supported by AWS for FIFO topics.'

Add a source caveat or reframe the FIFO throughput numbers as AWS-documented facts rather than Stacktape source-grounded figures (e.g., 'AWS documents FIFO SNS topic throughput at ~300 publishes/s...'). Alternatively, remove the numeric column and describe throughput qualitatively ('significantly lower than standard').

Add 1–2 sentences explaining memory and timeout in the Lambda subscriber example, or replace them with a link: 'Configure memory and timeout as you would for any Lambda function — see Lambda functions for supported ranges.'

Reorganize smsDisplayName as its own H2 ('SMS sender name' or 'SMS configuration') rather than nesting it under 'Standard vs FIFO topics'. This makes both sections individually extractable and removes the impression that SMS branding is a FIFO-related feature.

Expand the smsDisplayName section with decision guidance: when to set it (consumer-facing SMS alerts, regulated industries requiring sender identification), the default behavior when unset (AWS assigns a generic short code or sender ID by region), and the 11-character constraint's practical implication (abbreviations only).

issues.statement: Stacktape enforces this constraint — attempting to add an SNS trigger from a Lambda function to a FIFO topic will result in a deployment error.
issues.reason: The claim is factually correct — Stacktape does enforce this at the application level (error e86 in sns/index.ts). However, the phrase 'deployment error' is slightly misleading. Stacktape catches this BEFORE deployment, during configuration resolution. It throws an error during the stack calculation phase, not during CloudFormation deployment. The distinction matters because the user gets the error immediately, not after waiting for a deployment to fail.
issues.suggestedFix: Reword to: 'Stacktape validates this constraint — attempting to add an SNS trigger from a Lambda function to a FIFO topic will result in a configuration error before deployment begins.'

evidence.quote: if (topic.fifoEnabled) { throw stpErrors.e86({ snsTopicReferencerStpName: name, snsTopicReferencerStpType: configParentResourceType, snsTopicStpName: snsTopicName }); }

issues.statement: Stacktape grants SNS publish and subscribe permissions
issues.reason: This is correct but incomplete. The actual IAM policy grants six actions: sns:ConfirmSubscription, sns:GetTopicAttributes, sns:ListSubscriptionsByTopic, sns:Publish, sns:Subscribe, and sns:Unsubscribe. The draft's summary of 'publish and subscribe permissions' is a reasonable simplification for the reader and matches the connectTo JSDoc, but readers who need precise IAM knowledge should check the generated policy.
issues.suggestedFix: No change strictly needed — the phrasing matches the connectTo JSDoc. Optionally add a note that the full IAM policy includes read and management actions (GetTopicAttributes, ListSubscriptionsByTopic, ConfirmSubscription, Unsubscribe) beyond just Publish and Subscribe.

positiveFindings: All three SnsTopicProps properties (smsDisplayName, fifoEnabled, contentBasedDeduplication) are correctly documented with accurate defaults (fifoEnabled: false, contentBasedDeduplication: false).

The connectTo environment variable table correctly lists STP_NOTIFICATIONS_ARN and STP_NOTIFICATIONS_NAME, matching the SnsTopicReferencableParam union ('arn' | 'name') and the resource name 'notifications'.
The SnsIntegration trigger properties (snsTopicName, snsTopicArn, filterPolicy, onDeliveryFailure with sqsQueueName/sqsQueueArn) all match the events.d.ts source exactly.

The Standard vs FIFO comparison table accurately reflects source constraints: FIFO requires fifoEnabled: true, contentBasedDeduplication or per-message dedup IDs, and can only deliver to FIFO SQS queues.
'When to use' and 'When NOT to use' are correctly structured as peer H2 sections.
The ApiReference uses the correct definitionName 'SnsTopicProps' matching the source type definition.
The ReferenceableParams component uses the correct resource type 'sns-topic'.
All cross-links point to valid routes in the documentation navigation index.
The FAQ contains exactly 10 Q&A pairs spanning all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS service, and comparison questions).
Code examples use correct class names (SnsTopic, LambdaFunction, StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging) and proper CodeBlock intellisense format with 'TypeScript' label and focus markers.
verifierResults.summary: The draft is factually sound and well-grounded in the source files. All SnsTopicProps properties, defaults, connectTo environment variables, referenceable parameters, trigger integration properties, and cross-links are accurate. The two minor issues found are: (1) the FIFO enforcement warning says 'deployment error' when Stacktape actually catches this during configuration resolution before deployment begins, and (2) the connectTo permissions summary ('publish and subscribe') is a reasonable simplification of the six actual IAM actions granted, matching the connectTo JSDoc. Neither issue would cause a reader to misconfigure their stack. The page covers all union variants (standard vs FIFO), provides good decision support, and follows heading hierarchy correctly.

The draft is broadly aligned with the SNS topic type and trigger sources, but it makes several Stacktape-specific or source-scoped claims that are either unsupported or too broad. The main blockers are an incorrect FIFO subscriber constraint that omits Lambda, an unsupported claim that Stacktape enforces FIFO/Lambda subscription failures at deploy time, and a likely wrong ReferenceableParams resource key.

issues.statement: “FIFO topics can only deliver to FIFO SQS queues. Lambda, email, SMS, and HTTP subscribers are not supported with FIFO topics.” Similar claim appears in the FAQ: “FIFO topics can only deliver to FIFO SQS queues.”
issues.reason: The authoritative SnsTopic source says FIFO topics cannot deliver to email, SMS, or HTTP, but it does not exclude Lambda. Stating that FIFO topics can only deliver to FIFO SQS queues conflicts with the narrower parenthetical in the type source and would change how readers configure subscriptions.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the claim to the sourced wording: “FIFO topics can only deliver to FIFO SQS queues, and not to email, SMS, or HTTP subscribers.” Do not claim Lambda is unsupported unless a source file explicitly says so.

evidence.quote: FIFO topics can only deliver to FIFO SQS queues (not email, SMS, or HTTP).

issues.statement: “Stacktape enforces this constraint — attempting to add an SNS trigger from a Lambda function to a FIFO topic will result in a deployment error.”
issues.reason: The provided sources document the FIFO delivery constraint and the SNS trigger shape, but they do not show a Stacktape validator or deployment behavior for Lambda SNS triggers against FIFO topics. This is a concrete Stacktape behavior claim that could affect what readers expect during deployment.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the enforcement/deployment-error sentence, or replace it with a cautious source-grounded sentence such as: “The SnsTopic type documents FIFO delivery as limited to FIFO SQS queues, not email, SMS, or HTTP subscribers.”

evidence.quote: FIFO topics can only deliver to FIFO SQS queues (not email, SMS, or HTTP).

issues.statement: “The simplest SNS topic requires no properties at all. Stacktape creates a standard topic...”
issues.reason: The type source makes `properties` optional and `fifoEnabled` defaults to false, but it does not explicitly say that a class constructor call with an empty object is accepted or that the created topic is named “standard.” The behavior is likely intended, but the exact wording overstates what the source proves.
issues.suggestedFix: Write more narrowly: “An SNS topic has no required properties in the config type. Because `fifoEnabled` defaults to `false`, omitting FIFO settings creates the non-FIFO topic shape documented by the type.”

issues.statement: “Use `connectTo` on any compute resource (Lambda function, web service, worker service, etc.) to grant it publish access to the topic. Stacktape grants SNS publish and subscribe permissions...”
issues.reason: The source supports `connectTo` on workloads through `ResourceAccessProps`, but “any compute resource” is broader than the enumerated workload/resource types visible in the provided source. The source also says SNS Topic access is “publish/subscribe,” not just publish access, so the first sentence underspecifies the permission while the second sentence should stay tied to the source wording.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the source wording and avoid “any”: “Workloads that expose `connectTo`, such as Lambda functions and container workloads, can connect to an SNS topic. For `SnsTopic`, `connectTo` adds publish/subscribe permissions and injects `ARN` and `NAME` variables.”

issues.reason: The SNS topic source defines `SnsTopicReferencableParam`, but `StacktapeResourceReferenceableParam` in the provided helper source does not include `SnsTopicReferencableParam`. Without component-registry evidence, this resource key is not proven valid for the component and may render empty or fail validation.
issues.suggestedFix: Verify the ReferenceableParams registry before publishing. If SNS topics are not registered there, omit the component and list the two referenceable params in prose or fix the registry/source first.

evidence.quote:
type StacktapeResourceReferenceableParam =
  | ApplicationLoadBalancerReferenceableParam
  | BatchJobReferencableParam
  | BucketReferencableParam
  | ContainerWorkloadReferencableParam
  | DynamoDBTableReferencableParam
  | EventBusReferencableParam
  | FunctionReferencableParam
  | HttpApiGatewayReferencableParam
  | MongoDbAtlasClusterReferencableParam
  | RedisClusterReferencableParam
  | RelationalDatabaseReferencableParam
  | StateMachineReferencableParam
  | UpstashRedisReferencableParam
  | UserPoolReferencableParam
  | PrivateServiceReferencableParams
  | WebServiceReferencableParam
  | WorkerServiceReferencableParams
  | WebAppFirewallReferencableParams
  | OpenSearchDomainReferencableParams
  | KinesisStreamReferencableParam
  | ConvexReferencableParam;

issues.statement: “Standard topics cost less than FIFO topics per publish.” and “Standard topics cost less than FIFO topics per publish” in the pricing and FAQ sections.
issues.reason: The SNS source only says “Serverless, pay-per-message.” The general AWS pricing model can be useful context, but no provided source supports the standard-vs-FIFO price comparison or concrete Free Tier language. Because pricing is allowed as general AWS context, this is not a blocker, but the page should avoid presenting unsourced concrete pricing details as Stacktape-sourced facts.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep pricing high-level unless a pricing source is included: “SNS is pay-per-message, and SMS/email delivery can add separate charges. Check current AWS SNS pricing for regional details.”

issues.statement: “For SQS and HTTP subscribers, SNS retries according to its delivery policy.”
issues.reason: This is general AWS behavior, but the provided Stacktape sources only document `onDeliveryFailure` for SNS-triggered Lambda delivery failures and do not document SQS or HTTP subscriber delivery policies. The claim is not necessarily wrong, but it is outside the provided source set.
issues.suggestedFix: If keeping this AWS background claim, frame it explicitly as Amazon SNS behavior. Keep Stacktape-specific failure handling limited to `onDeliveryFailure` on the SNS trigger.

positiveFindings: The page uses `defineConfig` and class-based Stacktape config examples inside `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a `TypeScript` tab, with no hand-authored YAML config examples.
The `ApiReference` component uses `SnsTopicProps`, which matches the page resource props type in `types/stacktape-config/sns-topic.d.ts`.
The draft correctly states that SNS subscribers are configured on the subscriber side; this is directly supported by the SnsTopic source.
The `connectTo` environment variable names for a resource named `notifications` correctly follow the `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]` pattern and use the sourced SNS params `ARN` and `NAME`.

verifierResults.summary: API completeness audit found no publication-blocking or medium-priority completeness issues for the SNS Topic page. The draft covers every primary SnsTopicProps property, acknowledges the relevant standard/FIFO choice, and the Stacktape config examples demonstrate the sections they appear in.
positiveFindings: All primary SnsTopicProps properties are covered: smsDisplayName, fifoEnabled, and contentBasedDeduplication.
The page includes exactly one API reference and uses the correct definitionName: SnsTopicProps.
Stacktape config examples use CodeBlock with the required TypeScript tab rather than fenced TypeScript or hand-authored YAML.

Feature examples are demonstrative: FIFO examples set fifoEnabled/contentBasedDeduplication, SMS examples set smsDisplayName, Lambda subscription examples set an sns event, and connectTo examples set connectTo.
The connectTo environment variables use the concrete resource name notifications, producing STP_NOTIFICATIONS_ARN and STP_NOTIFICATIONS_NAME, which matches the documented naming pattern.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: No mandatory style violations — all Stacktape config examples use <CodeBlock intellisense> with TypeScript, no hand-authored YAML, no StacktapeConfig import, correct class names throughout.
Heading style perfectly matches the example: noun-form H2s, same ordering for shared sections, correct H2 order relative to the SQS example page.
Frontmatter keys match the example exactly (title, order, seoTitle, seoDescription).
MDX component palette matches: <Warning> and <Tip> used, same as the example; no extra callout types introduced.
<ApiReference definitionName="SnsTopicProps" /> placement matches example (after Referenceable parameters), correct type name from source.
Standard vs FIFO comparison table is clear and well-structured — covers ordering, delivery, subscriber types, throughput, and use cases.
connectTo section is accurate: ARN and NAME env vars match the __helpers.d.ts JSDoc exactly for SnsTopic, with correctly scoped example using resource name 'notifications'.
Fan-out pattern section shows a realistic two-Lambda-subscriber example with a working publish-from-Lambda code snippet using the correct AWS SDK pattern.
FAQ correctly covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific, AWS service behavior, and comparison/choosing questions, with exactly 10 Q&As.
Correct omission of Alarms section — SnsTopic is not in StpAlarmEnabledResource, so omitting it is the right call and matches the source-grounding verifier discipline.
problems: The FIFO throughput figures (~300 publishes/s, 3,000 with batching) appear both in the comparison table and in the FAQ answer for message ordering, but are not present in any provided source file. These are well-known AWS facts but the source-grounding verifier will flag them. They should be framed as 'AWS-side limits' rather than Stacktape-specific claims, and softened with 'approximately' or a note that limits may vary.

The smsDisplayName subsection is thin: it shows a working example but provides no real decision-support. A developer reading it won't know when to set it vs. skip it, or what the consequences of not setting it are. The section lacks a 'when to enable / when the default is fine' signal.

The SNS-to-SQS fan-out limitation (requires raw CloudFormation for SQS subscriptions) is buried inside a <Tip> block within the Fan-out section. For many readers this pattern is the primary reason to use SNS, so discovering it requires raw CloudFormation mid-section — after already reading a fan-out example — is jarring. The limitation deserves clearer positioning.

The 'Subscribing a Lambda function' section mentions filterPolicy as a key setting but provides no code example. The parallel SQS section shows batchSize and maxBatchWindowSeconds in the example code — the SNS section should show filterPolicy inline (at minimum a short snippet) to give the reader a concrete reference, since filter policies are non-obvious in syntax.
optionalImprovements: In the Standard vs FIFO comparison table and in FAQ #10, attribute the throughput figures (~300 publishes/s) explicitly as AWS-side limits: 'AWS limits FIFO topics to approximately 300 publishes/s (3,000 with batching)' — this frames them as background facts rather than Stacktape claims.

Expand the smsDisplayName subsection: add 1–2 sentences covering when to set it (user-facing mobile notifications where sender branding matters), when to skip it (internal system alerts where the default 'NOTICE' sender is fine), and a note that the 11-character limit means abbreviations are often necessary.

Promote the SNS-to-SQS fan-out limitation to a more prominent position. Consider adding an explicit sentence before the <Tip> stating: 'Stacktape does not currently have a first-class SQS subscription type for SNS topics — wiring an SQS queue as an SNS subscriber requires a raw CloudFormation resource.' This way the reader isn't surprised mid-section.

Add a minimal filterPolicy code example inside the 'Subscribing a Lambda function' section, showing what an attribute-based filter looks like (e.g., filtering on an 'eventType' attribute). One short snippet next to the key settings list would turn an abstract concept into something immediately usable.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Heading form and H2 order match the example (noun-form, same shared-section sequence: When to use → When NOT to use → Basic example → Standard vs FIFO → ... → Connecting to other resources → Referenceable params → API Reference → FAQ).
Fan-out pattern section provides a realistic two-Lambda example (emailNotifier + analyticsProcessor on the same topic) that shows the primary SNS use case — not just a toy config.
All CodeBlock examples use `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} />` with correct label:'TypeScript', focus markers, and hidden-import pattern. No forbidden YAML or fenced-ts violations.
connectTo env var table correctly uses resource-name-dependent names (STP_NOTIFICATIONS_ARN, STP_NOTIFICATIONS_NAME) matched to the example resource named 'notifications'.
FIFO topic Warning correctly flags the FIFO→FIFO-SQS-only subscriber constraint sourced from the type JSDoc.
Lambda handler snippet uses the correct SNS event shape (Records[].Sns.Message, Records[].Sns.MessageId).
FAQ has exactly 10 Q&As with good coverage across all three required categories: Stacktape-specific, AWS use-case, and choosing/comparison questions.

Sections legitimately absent from the SNS source (alarms — SnsTopic is not in StpAlarmEnabledResource; EventBridge subscription — SnsTopicProps has no events property) are correctly omitted without leaving placeholder headings.
problems: 'SMS display name' is a H3 nested under the 'Standard vs FIFO topics' section but has no logical relationship to the FIFO vs standard comparison. It reads as a misplaced property note rather than a natural sub-topic of the section it lives in.

The critical limitation that SNS-to-SQS fan-out (the canonical cloud pattern) requires raw CloudFormation in Stacktape is buried inside a `<Tip>` callout in the 'Fan-out pattern' section. A reader skimming that section for implementation guidance may miss it; it should be stated clearly in the prose before the tip.

The opening pricing line ('The first 1 million publishes per month are in the AWS Free Tier') is not grounded in any of the provided source files — sns-topic.d.ts and the AI-generated config-ref both say 'pay-per-message' without citing the free tier threshold. The example SQS page includes pricing because it was presumably in that page's source files; this page lacks that source grounding.

The 'Subscribing a Lambda function' section duplicates structural content from 'Fan-out pattern' — both sections show a Lambda subscribing with type:'sns'/snsTopicName. A reader following the page linearly will see the same subscription pattern twice with different framing. Consider merging or differentiating them more clearly.
optionalImprovements: Move the 'SMS display name' H3 out of 'Standard vs FIFO topics'. Either fold it into the comparison table as a row (Standard supports SMS, FIFO does not) or handle it with a brief inline prose note inside the Standard section rather than a separate H3.

Elevate the SNS→SQS fan-out limitation from a <Tip> to a sentence in the prose before the tip: 'For subscribers that need independent buffering and retry, the common AWS pattern routes SNS to multiple SQS queues — each queue feeds one consumer. In Stacktape this requires a raw CloudFormation AWS::SNS::Subscription resource because SnsTopicProps does not expose a native subscriptions property.' Then keep the Tip for the link.

Guard the free-tier pricing claim or soften it: change 'The first 1 million publishes per month are in the AWS Free Tier' to 'Amazon SNS includes a monthly free tier for publishes — check the current AWS SNS pricing page for exact thresholds.' This avoids asserting a specific figure not present in the provided source files.

The 'Fan-out pattern' and 'Subscribing a Lambda function' sections can be collapsed. 'Fan-out' is the why (multiple subscribers), 'Subscribing a Lambda function' is the how (single subscriber trigger config + handler). Consider making 'Subscribing a Lambda function' a H3 under 'Fan-out pattern' or merging the handler snippet into the fan-out section and removing the duplicate section.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Strong heading structure that matches the example page: noun-form H2s in the same order as shared sections, with new SNS-specific sections (Fan-out pattern, Subscribing a Lambda function) inserted at appropriate positions.
Comparison table for Standard vs FIFO topics is fact-dense and citable — ordering, delivery guarantees, subscriber types, throughput, and use cases all in one scannable view.
All code examples use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}> with label: 'TypeScript', focus markers, and hidden imports — fully compliant with the style guide.
connectTo env-var table uses the correct resource-name-dependent naming convention (STP_NOTIFICATIONS_ARN, STP_NOTIFICATIONS_NAME) derived from the example resource name.

10-question FAQ covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (filterPolicy, ordering, smsDisplayName), AWS service use-case (pricing, message size, delivery failure), and comparison/choosing (SNS vs SQS, SNS vs EventBridge).
When NOT to use section gives specific, concrete alternatives for each anti-pattern rather than vague guidance.

Source-grounded claims throughout: fifoEnabled default false, contentBasedDeduplication SHA-256 within 5-minute window, FIFO subscriber restriction to FIFO SQS queues — all match types/stacktape-config/sns-topic.d.ts.
Correctly omits Alarms and EventBridge integration sections — SNS topic is absent from StpAlarmEnabledResource and SnsTopicProps has no events property.
Opening paragraph is 60 words, self-contained, answer-first — matches the example's density and structure.
problems: The Fan-out pattern section ends with a <Tip> suggesting SNS→SQS fan-out requires raw CloudFormation resources, but provides no code sketch or link deep enough for a reader to act on it. This is the most common SNS pattern in production AWS architectures; leaving it as a one-liner tip without even a pointer to which CloudFormation resource type to use leaves users stranded.

filterPolicy and onDeliveryFailure are listed as 'key settings' in the Subscribing a Lambda function section but neither receives a code example or tradeoff explanation. filterPolicy in particular is a significant feature (reduces Lambda invocations and cost) that deserves at least one illustrative example showing the attribute-filter syntax.

The FIFO throughput numbers (~300 publishes/s, 3,000 with batching) appear in the comparison table and FAQ but are not grounded in the provided source files. The source only describes FIFO for 'financial transactions, sequential workflows' without any throughput figures. Readers may accept these numbers as Stacktape-stated facts; a well-known AWS fact caveat would make this safer.

The SMS delivery section (smsDisplayName) is a standalone <CodeBlock> with minimal surrounding prose. It states what the property does but gives no context for when SMS delivery is useful, what AWS account-level prerequisites exist, or what the cost implications are — failing the decision-support criterion for opt-in features.

SnsTopicReferencableParam (arn | name) is defined in the source but is absent from the StacktapeResourceReferenceableParam union in __helpers.d.ts. If the <ReferenceableParams resource='sns-topic' /> component relies on that union, the section may render empty. This should be verified before publication.
optionalImprovements: Expand the SNS→SQS fan-out Tip into a short sub-section or richer callout: name the CloudFormation resource type (AWS::SNS::Subscription), explain when to prefer this pattern over pure Lambda fan-out (buffering, independent retry), and link to the raw CloudFormation resources page with a concrete property snippet.

Add a filterPolicy code example under Subscribing a Lambda function showing a realistic attribute filter (e.g., filtering by event-type attribute). A two-sentence explanation of how attribute-based filtering differs from EventBridge content-based routing would round out the decision-support for the feature.

Add a one-sentence caveat to the FIFO throughput row in the comparison table — e.g., 'Based on AWS SNS documentation; verify current limits in the AWS SNS quotas page' — to prevent the ungrounded numbers from being cited as Stacktape-authoritative facts.

Expand the smsDisplayName sub-section with 2-3 sentences of context: typical use cases (transactional alerts, OTP codes), the fact that SMS delivery requires AWS account-level configuration for some destination countries (sandbox mode), and a note that FIFO topics do not support SMS (already stated elsewhere but worth repeating here at the point of configuration).

Verify that <ReferenceableParams resource='sns-topic' /> renders correctly at build time given that SnsTopicReferencableParam is not present in the StacktapeResourceReferenceableParam union in __helpers.d.ts. If it renders empty, either document the two parameters (arn, name) inline as a simple table, or confirm the component sources data from a different registry.

Consider adding a brief 'Policy statements' note (analogous to the Queue policies section in the SQS example) clarifying that SNS topics do not expose a policyStatements property in Stacktape config — readers migrating from the SQS page will expect it and may search for it.

issues.statement: The SMS display name section consists of a one-sentence definition, a code example, and no decision support — no when-to-use scenario, no cost/tradeoff signal, no mention of AWS account-level SMS sandbox requirements.
issues.reason: Per documentation-quality rules, every opt-in feature section must cover at least 3 of: when-to-enable, when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff, opinion. The SMS section has none of these — it jumps from definition straight to code. SMS in particular has meaningful cost and regulatory context (per-message charges vary by country, accounts start in SMS sandbox, destination-country opt-in requirements) that readers need before enabling it.
issues.suggestedFix: Add 2-3 sentences before the code example: when SMS makes sense (transactional alerts to phone numbers, OTP flows), that SMS carries separate per-message charges that vary by destination country, that new AWS accounts start in an SMS sandbox requiring individual number verification, and that most teams skip SMS in favor of email or webhook notifications unless the use case specifically requires it.

issues.statement: The FIFO topic section mentions content-based deduplication and MessageDeduplicationId but does not mention MessageGroupId, which is required for FIFO ordering.
issues.reason: FIFO SNS topics require a MessageGroupId on every publish to determine ordering scope. This is a well-known AWS FIFO requirement. Omitting it means a reader who enables FIFO may hit errors on their first publish. While this is general AWS knowledge rather than a Stacktape-specific claim, it's directly relevant to the decision to use fifoEnabled: true.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a brief note in the FIFO section or the FAQ answer about ordering: 'When publishing to a FIFO topic, you must include a MessageGroupId to define the ordering scope — messages within the same group are delivered in order.'

evidence.quote: Requires either `contentBasedDeduplication: true` or a unique `MessageDeduplicationId` per message.

issues.statement: The fan-out pattern Tip says 'In Stacktape, you can set this up using raw CloudFormation resources to create the AWS::SNS::Subscription linking each queue to the topic.'
issues.reason: While technically correct, this guidance could be stronger. The reader may not know how to wire an SNS-to-SQS subscription via raw CloudFormation, and the link to /resources/advanced/raw-cloudformation-resources is present but the Tip doesn't mention that the SQS queue also needs an IAM policy allowing SNS to send messages. This is the most common pitfall with SNS→SQS fan-out via raw CF.
issues.suggestedFix: Either add a brief note that the SQS queue's access policy must allow sns:SendMessage from the topic ARN, or simplify the tip to just describe the pattern conceptually without implying it's straightforward via raw CF.

positiveFindings: All property names (fifoEnabled, contentBasedDeduplication, smsDisplayName) and their defaults (@default false) match the source type definitions exactly.

The connectTo env-var table correctly lists STP_NOTIFICATIONS_ARN and STP_NOTIFICATIONS_NAME, matching the source JSDoc pattern 'SnsTopic — publish/subscribe → ARN, NAME' with the concrete resource name 'notifications'.
The SnsTopicReferencableParam type ('arn' | 'name') is correctly reflected in both the connectTo table and the <ReferenceableParams resource='sns-topic' /> component.
'When to use' and 'When NOT to use' are correctly structured as peer H2 sections, not nested.
<ApiReference definitionName='SnsTopicProps' /> correctly uses the Props-suffixed type name matching the source interface.
All SNS trigger properties (snsTopicName, snsTopicArn, filterPolicy, onDeliveryFailure) are accurately described and match the SnsIntegrationProps source type.

All code examples use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> with label: 'TypeScript' for Stacktape config, and fenced code blocks for application code (handler). No forbidden YAML or fenced-TypeScript patterns.
FAQ section has exactly 10 Q&A pairs spanning all three required categories: Stacktape-specific, AWS service use-case, and choosing/comparison questions.

All cross-links (/resources/compute/lambda-function, /resources/messaging/sqs-queue, /configuration/triggers/sns-events, /configuration/connecting-resources, etc.) are valid routes from the navigation index.
FIFO topic constraints (FIFO SQS queues only, deduplication requirement, contentBasedDeduplication SHA-256 within 5-minute window) all match source JSDoc verbatim.

Standard vs FIFO comparison table provides concrete decision support with ordering guarantees, delivery semantics, subscriber restrictions, and throughput — well-grounded in source and general AWS knowledge.
verifierResults.summary: The SNS Topic draft is well-grounded and factually accurate. All Stacktape-specific claims — property names, defaults, connectTo env-var injections, referenceable parameters, trigger configuration, and component usage — match the source type definitions. The page structure follows all hard requirements: peer H2 sections for when-to-use/when-not-to-use, correct ApiReference definitionName with Props suffix, proper CodeBlock usage, and a complete 10-item FAQ. No high-severity issues found. The three issues raised are quality-depth concerns: the SMS display name section needs decision support, the FIFO section would benefit from mentioning MessageGroupId, and the SNS-to-SQS fan-out tip could note the SQS access policy requirement.

The draft is mostly grounded in the provided SNS topic, SNS trigger, and connectTo sources. The main publication-risk issues are two Stacktape-specific claims that overstate what the source proves: direct non-Lambda subscription support in Stacktape configuration and the exact SNS trigger failure scenario.

issues.statement: "Standard SNS topics can deliver to Lambda functions, SQS queues, HTTP/HTTPS endpoints, email addresses, and SMS phone numbers." and the page repeatedly implies these subscriber types are configurable directly through Stacktape, while only Lambda subscriptions via SNS triggers are documented in the provided Stacktape source.
issues.reason: The SnsTopic source describes SNS as a service that can deliver to Lambda, SQS, email, SMS, and HTTP, but the Stacktape configuration surface shown in the provided files only documents SNS subscriptions through `SnsIntegration`, which triggers a function. The draft later correctly says SQS fan-out needs raw CloudFormation, but the earlier broad phrasing may make readers think Stacktape exposes first-class subscription configuration for all subscriber types on the topic resource.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the Stacktape-specific wording: say the underlying SNS topic supports Lambda, SQS, email, SMS, and HTTP delivery, while Stacktape's first-class trigger shown here is Lambda via `events: [{ type: 'sns' }]`; other subscription shapes may require CloudFormation or another AWS-side setup unless documented elsewhere.

evidence.quote: Subscribers are configured on the subscriber side.
#### Triggers a function when a new message is published to an SNS topic.

issues.statement: "If all retries fail, the message is lost unless you configure `onDeliveryFailure` on the SNS trigger to route failed messages to an SQS dead-letter queue for later inspection."
issues.reason: The provided Stacktape source documents `onDeliveryFailure` only for messages that fail to be delivered to the SNS trigger target, with the example cause being target function scaling. It does not support the broader claim that `onDeliveryFailure` preserves messages after all SNS delivery retries for every offline subscriber case.
issues.suggestedFix: Scope this to Lambda SNS triggers: "For Stacktape SNS triggers, `onDeliveryFailure` can send messages that fail to be delivered to the target function to an SQS queue." Avoid tying it to all subscriber-offline scenarios.

evidence.quote:
In rare cases (e.g., if the target function cannot scale fast enough), a message might fail to be delivered.
   * This property specifies an SQS queue where failed messages will be sent.

issues.statement: "Set `smsDisplayName` to customize the sender name shown on SMS messages ... This only applies to standard topics — FIFO topics do not support SMS delivery."
issues.reason: The conclusion is probably true from the combination of `smsDisplayName` and FIFO subscriber constraints, but the `smsDisplayName` property itself is not explicitly scoped to standard topics in the provided type definition. This is a low-risk wording issue because the FIFO source says FIFO topics cannot deliver to SMS.
issues.suggestedFix: Phrase it as an inference: "Because FIFO topics can only deliver to FIFO SQS queues, `smsDisplayName` is only useful for standard topics that send SMS."

positiveFindings: The draft uses class-based `defineConfig` examples inside `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with the required `label: 'TypeScript'` and does not hand-author YAML Stacktape config.
`<ApiReference definitionName="SnsTopicProps" />` matches the page resource type and includes the `Props` suffix.
The connectTo environment variable names for the `notifications` resource correctly follow `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]`: `STP_NOTIFICATIONS_ARN` and `STP_NOTIFICATIONS_NAME`.

The draft correctly documents `fifoEnabled` defaulting to `false`, `contentBasedDeduplication` defaulting to `false`, FIFO requiring content-based deduplication or `MessageDeduplicationId`, and SNS topic referenceable parameters `arn` and `name`.

verifierResults.summary: The draft covers all primary `SnsTopicProps` properties and the documented SNS trigger settings it chooses to discuss. I did not find API-completeness, union-variant, or code-example demonstrativeness issues that meet the reporting threshold.
positiveFindings: All primary `SnsTopicProps` fields are documented: `smsDisplayName`, `fifoEnabled`, and `contentBasedDeduplication`.
The page correctly includes exactly one `<ApiReference definitionName="SnsTopicProps" />`.
Each Stacktape configuration example uses `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a `TypeScript` tab and demonstrates the section topic it appears under.
The Lambda subscription section documents both same-stack and external-topic paths via `snsTopicName` and `snsTopicArn`, plus `filterPolicy` and `onDeliveryFailure`.
The page includes `<ReferenceableParams resource="sns-topic" />` and documents the `arn` and `name` referenceable parameters through `connectTo` environment variables.
